request.freeText: I spend way too much on food

response.scoreModifierReason: Near-zero liquid savings in a VHCOL state with a known overspending pattern leaves essentially no buffer for any disruption.
response.summary: Full-time employed in CA with almost nothing saved and a self-admitted food spending problem — the income bracket is workable but the savings cushion is basically nonexistent. No debt is a real win, but one bad month away from going into the red. The food habit is the most actionable lever right now.
response.roast: You're in California, employed, debt-free, and somehow your savings account has less in it than a Chipotle order for two. The food budget called — it said it's the main character and you're just the wallet.
insights: With under $500 saved in a VHCOL state, you have essentially zero runway — one unexpected bill and you're in the red.
No debt is genuinely rare and valuable — don't let food spending slowly build a credit card balance to compensate.
Food overspending is often the most fixable leak: meal prepping or a weekly cap can recover $100–$200/mo fast.
At your income bracket, a 15% savings rate means setting aside ~$675/mo — right now you're nowhere near that.
The gap between your income and savings suggests most of your paycheck is gone before you notice it leaving.
topProblems: Near-zero emergency fund in a high cost-of-living state — one surprise away from debt.
Self-identified food overspending draining money that should be building a cushion.
Savings rate is effectively 0%, leaving no progress toward financial security.
positiveBehaviors: Zero debt is a massive structural advantage — you have no interest payments eating your income.
You're self-aware enough to name the problem, which is further than most people get.

topFix.action: Set a hard weekly food budget, track every purchase for 30 days, and redirect whatever you recover straight into a high-yield savings account until you hit one month of expenses.
